title: ‘Stuart Saves the Universe’ Producers on Pulling Off Such a Crazy ‘Big Bang Theory’ Spinoff After ‘No One Really Liked the Idea’
description: As his megahit CBS sitcom “The Big Bang Theory” prepared to end its run, Chuck Lorre came up with a wild pitch for a spinoff. Not “Young Sheldon” —
body:
As his megahit CBS sitcom “The Big Bang Theory” prepared to end its run, Chuck Lorre came up with a wild pitch for a spinoff. Not “Young Sheldon” — that prequel was already, on the air and doing gangbusters. No, Lorre had something even more fantastical in mind.

	“I had a strange idea that there was a portal in the back of the comic book store that led to other dimensions, and that Stuart, played by Kevin Sussman, could lead us on an action adventure-comedy-science-fiction mashup,” he tells Variety.
	

	But here’s the rub: “No one really liked the idea,” he admits. Hard to believe, especially coming right off one of the most successful TV comedies of all time, that someone like Lorre would face a network and studio rejection. But Lorre says those programming execs were looking for something a bit more conventional and a little less — well, whatever it was that Lorre had conjured.


	“Everybody wanted a spinoff of ‘The Big Bang Theory,’” he says. “They wanted it with the principal players, which is understandable. I didn’t want to do a spinoff with the principal players. I wanted to do something entirely different, and challenging — something I’d never done before. Which is certainly what we have now.”

	What they have now is HBO Max’s 10-episode “Stuart Fails to Save the Universe,” which premieres July 23 on the platform. After the initial pitch was a pass, Lorre temporarily shelved the idea, keeping busy with series like “The Kominsky Method” and “Bookie.” But he never gave up on “Stuart,” bringing in “Big Bang” co-creator Bill Prady and sci-fi-comic expert Zak Penn (“The Avengers,” “The Incredible Hulk”) to hone the concept and write the script.
	

	“Chuck recruited me sometime during the pandemic,” Penn says. “The thing that grabbed me was from the very beginning, he said, ‘I want to do a spinoff that’s not like any other spinoff you’ve seen,’ where the tone and the genre shift. He wanted to do something that was in the sci-fi fantasy realm, so that’s up my alley. It was an easy yes.”

	As for Prady, “my favorite episodes of ‘Star Trek’ were always the comedic episodes, and so the idea of playing in comedy science fiction was like, ‘Let’s figure this out.’”

	Meanwhile, just in case it might actually happen, Lorre had kept Sussman on standby. “He wanted to pitch me the idea of a spinoff about Stuart,” Sussman says, “which is weird because, does that mean he was imagining I might say ‘no’?”

	Years later, Lorre, Penn and Prady finally came up with a spinoff pitch that secured a greenlight from Warner Bros. TV and HBO Max. “That surprised the heck out of me,” says Lorre.

	In “The Big Bang Theory,” Sussman’s comic book store owner, Stuart Bloom, was a minor player. But he’s front and center in “Stuart Fails to Save the Universe,” as he and his friends bounce through the multiverse after a quantum interference device created by “Big Bang”’s Leonard, Sheldon and Howard (referenced, but not seen) malfunctions. In this new alt-reality, Stuart and his comic book pals contend with alternate, uniquely dangerous versions of the shop and its Pasadena environs.

	And that doesn’t begin to describe how far-out the show gets. In one episode, a familiar face is turned into a 60-foot witch. In another, one of the stars turns into a magical wizard. Each episode is its own story and setting — and as the title hints, Stuart never manages to make things right.

	“It’s so outside of the box, and it’s so not like the original show,” says Sussman. “It’s a huge risk in terms of doing something completely different, but on the other side, it is still Chuck Lorre, and it is based on beloved IP. I love that he took such a big swing, because I don’t want to have to be compared to the original multi-cam.”

	Besides Sussman, leading the show are fellow “Big Bang” alums Lauren Lapkus (as Stuart’s love interest, Denise), Brian Posehn (Bert) and John Ross Bowie (Kripke). But plenty of other “Big Bang” players make cameos as multiverse versions of their characters — and even recurring faces from the original series, like Wil Wheaton (once again as himself), show up. Then there’s a new character, Gary (Tommy Walker), a potential rival for Denise’s affections in the show’s various worlds.
	

	It’s a long way from the multi-camera, studio-audience comforts of “The Big Bang Theory.” For one thing, that show never featured any gunshots, explosions — or a high, gruesome body count, for that matter. But “Stuart Fails to Save the Universe” sure does. At one point during production, Sussman and Lapkus were latched onto cables and hoisted to the ceiling of a Warner Bros. soundstage. And then they were dropped. Beneath them, huge industrial fans were blowing to make it look like they were plummeting through a void.

	“In the script, we’re supposed to be screaming,” Sussman says. “But Lauren and I both thought the cable broke and that we were just lunging toward a giant fan. Rather than screaming, it was almost like we couldn’t get any air, and we were struggling to squish out some sound.”

	With its special effects, travel through space and time and bloody deaths along the way, “Stuart” is living up to Lorre’s desire to make it one of the most unusual TV spinoffs of all time.

	For one thing, the VFX costs on “Stuart” were unlike anything Lorre had previously spent on any of his other shows. But the producers figured out a way to make the series work, and they even brought the show in under budget. (It probably helps that “Stuart” doesn’t come with the pricey salaries for talent that “Big Bang” eventually faced in success.)

	The producers also credit shooting in Los Angeles, thanks to the state of California tax credit. Filming locally allowed them to hire a top-tier crew who could pull off the show’s stunts and effects on a dime, they said.

	“It’s much easier when you shoot in L.A. to get the cream of the crop in every department, because creating a good effects-driven show is not just about the visual effects,” Penn says. “It’s about the production design. It’s about the cinematography. I also think they gave us a good enough budget to go out and create real special effects.”

	Prady notes that the science is legit on “Stuart” — or as legit as it can be for a sci-fi comedy about a quantum interference device that malfunctions and causes our characters to fall through the multiverse. ”That moment happened in the prime universe, but there’s no episode that takes place of these 10 in the prime universe. So is it canon? It’s multiverse canon!” he says. Lorre retorts with a joke: “Don’t poke at it too much!”
	

	There are “Big Bang” Easter eggs all over “Stuart,” which Lorre says makes sense: “We’ve long determined that this is the kind of show that the characters on ‘The Big Bang Theory’ would have not only watched, they would have argued about,” he says. At the same time, Sussman doesn’t think you need to have been a “Big Bang” fan before watching “Stuart.” “I feel like they did a really good job of orchestrating these three four characters,” he says.

	One element familiar to Lorre sitcom fans is back: His famed vanity cards, where Lorre takes the opportunity to converse with his viewers. As he makes clear at the end of Episode 2: “Throughout the making of ‘SFTSTY,’ my mantra was ‘Go crazy or go home.’ As you can see, I didn’t go home.”

schema.headline: ‘Stuart Saves the Universe’ Producers on Pulling Off Such a Crazy ‘Big Bang Theory’ Spinoff After ‘No One Really Liked the Idea’

title: Nexstar Accused of Violating Injunction by Appointing Tegna Board
description: A coalition of 13 attorneys general joined with DirecTV on Wednesday in accusing Nexstar of violating an injunction by appointing the members of Teg
body:
A coalition of 13 attorneys general joined with DirecTV on Wednesday in accusing Nexstar of violating an injunction by appointing the members of Tegna‘s board.

	The two TV station groups were ordered in April to halt their integration and to maintain independent operations pending the outcome of an antitrust trial. But Nexstar has since appointed its own current and former executives to serve on Tegna’s board, which the plaintiffs allege is not allowed under the order.
	

	“Nexstar cannot simultaneously control TEGNA’s Board and comply with an injunction requiring TEGNA to remain independent,” the plaintiffs wrote. “This should have been obvious.”


	The plaintiffs asked Judge Troy Nunley, who issued the order, to clarify that it does not permit Nexstar to oversee Tegna’s operations in this way.

	In response, Nexstar said it has “scrupulously complied with the Court’s hold-separate order.”

	“TEGNA continues to operate independently, and Nexstar has no involvement in TEGNA’s retransmission consent negotiations, content decisions, staffing, or other day-to-day operations,” the company said. “Nexstar’s executives’ service on TEGNA’s Board is consistent with the Court’s order and is critical to ensuring that Nexstar can continue to satisfy its financial reporting obligations while the hold-separate requirements are in place.”

	Nexstar has appealed the injunction to the 9th Circuit, and is awaiting a ruling.

	In their motion, the plaintiffs point to comments that Nexstar CEO Perry Sook made in May, in which he said that Tegna would operate as a subsidiary of Nexstar, and that the managers would report to the board.
	

	Those comments led the plaintiffs to seek further information about how the companies are being run. The motion alleges that Nexstar has refused to turn over certain information or respond to questions, and has likewise refused to remove its executives from the Tegna board.

	“The stakes here are high: Further integration is against the court’s directive and, if allowed, would kick off Nexstar and Tegna’s ability to control and raise prices, fire journalists, and dominate the broadcast media landscape,” said California Attorney General Rob Bonta, in a statement.

	The motion also asks the judge to order expedited discovery and monthly updates to the court about how Tegna is being run.

	The states allege that the merger will give Nexstar undue leverage in retransmission negotiations with cable and satellite distributors, leading to higher consumer prices. The suit also alleges that the merger will lead to fewer independent local news outlets, “at a time when, with challenges to local newspapers, local broadcast news coverage is critical to the ability of an informed citizenry to participate in localgovernmental and community activities.”

schema.headline: Nexstar Accused of Violating Injunction by Appointing Tegna Board

title: Hollywood’s Favorite Ugg Slippers (To Never Wear on a Christopher Nolan Set)
description:
If you purchase an independently reviewed product or service through a link on our website, Variety may receive an affiliate commission.

	Christoph
body:
If you purchase an independently reviewed product or service through a link on our website, Variety may receive an affiliate commission.

	Christopher Nolan‘s “The Odyssey” has spawned countless debates since its blockbuster release  — over representation and historical accuracy and, at least in my friend group, whether Travis Scott has iPhone face. But another, albeit much more benign, question has emerged during the press cycle this week: can an actor convincingly play an Ancient Greek god in the presence of an Ugg slipper?

	This is perhaps the first time you’ve seen those words strung together but Nolan has long banned Uggs from his sets. In an interview with Gayle King on Tuesday, he described the popular shoe as a “signifier of modernity” that “can actually weirdly take you out of reality.”


	In the case of “The Odyssey,” it makes sense that Nolan may not have wanted Matt Damon’s Odysseus, running miles through Troy in sandals, to have the comfort of sheepskin slippers in the back of his mind. Penelope, too, would surely have appreciated Ugg’s signature plush lining during her 20-year wait in the palace of Ithaca, but it may have taken Anne Hathaway out of the moment.

	Thankfully, in the modern world, one doesn’t have to be a queen to enjoy such luxuries. “Sometimes actors will turn up, because the footwear hurts them, in these big, fluffy, comfy slippers,” Nolan said, adding that his shoe ban has become a long-running joke with Emily Blunt and Anne Hathaway — two Ugg devotees who have had to set aside their loyalties while working with the Oscar-winning director.

	If you’re a Nolan-head now finding yourself eyeing your own Ugg boots in despair: worry not. “Uggs are great,” he told King when asked if he had something against the brand in particular. My middle school self, who treated her Uggs with as much care as Carrie Bradshaw does her Manolos, is also glowing in his approval.


	Hollywood has long had a love affair with Uggs, which were founded in 1978 in Santa Monica, California. Kate Moss, Paris Hilton, Leonardo DiCaprio and Sarah Jessica Parker are just a few of their most famous fans. Other sheepskin sightings? Beyonce at the Super Bowl, Ashley Tisdale on a red carpet, Ben Affleck on a (non-Nolan set) and Sienna Miller at the airport. They may be a “signifier of modernity,” but they’re also impressively versatile…

	Below, shop a few of Hollywood’s favorite Ugg styles through the years:


						
			
	

	
					


	

	
					


	

	
					


	

	
					

		

						
			
	

	
					
		

										DUPE						

					Fireside by Dearfoams Perth Bootie

schema.headline: Hollywood’s Favorite Ugg Slippers (To Never Wear on a Christopher Nolan Set)

title: YouTube Ad Sales Keep Growing at Healthy Clip, Rising 13% to $11 Billion in Q2
description:
Video giant YouTube, one of the world’s biggest entertainment businesses, continues to turn in double-digit increases in ad revenue.

	YouTube poste
body:
Video giant YouTube, one of the world’s biggest entertainment businesses, continues to turn in double-digit increases in ad revenue.

	YouTube posted ad sales of $11.06 billion for the second quarter of 2026, up 12.6% year over year. The results exceeded Wall Street analysts expectations for ad revenue of $10.8 billion, per StreetAccount. That doesn’t include YouTube’s billions of dollars in subscription revenue; in 2025, total YouTube revenue topped $60 billion, according to parent company Alphabet.
	

	Overall, Alphabet, which is also the parent of Google, posted Q2 revenue of $119.8 billion (up 24%) and net income of $112.1 billion (up nearly fourfold), or $9.11 per share, smashing analyst estimates. The company’s Q2 net income included a whopping $99 billion net gain on equity securities during the period; Alphabet’s holdings include investments in Anthropic and SpaceX.


	In announcing the earnings, Sundar Pichai, CEO of Google and Alphabet, commented that “month over month, people turn to YouTube for major world events, with over 1.7 billion unique viewers watching World Cup-related videos during the FIFA World Cup 2026.”

	Pichai called out the AI-driven “Ask YouTube” feature, which uses Google Gemini models to let users ask “complex questions” about individual videos, get quick takeaways and jump to specific points in a clip. In June 2026, more than 140 million users engaged with Ask YouTube on the Watch page, he said.

	Providing a big boost to Alphabet’s top line was Google Cloud, where Q2 revenue jumped 82%, to $24.77 billion. That was “driven by demand for AI infrastructure and AI solutions,” Pichai said. Operating income in the Google Cloud segment tripled, to $8.81 billion.
	

	Alphabet’s capital expenditures doubled in the quarter, to $44.9 billion, as the company continued to heavily invest in building out AI capacity.

schema.headline: YouTube Ad Sales Keep Growing at Healthy Clip, Rising 13% to $11 Billion in Q2

title: Bose Wants to Be the Centerpiece of Your Living Room Again With the Lifestyle Ultra Speaker
description:
If you purchase an independently reviewed product or service through a link on our website, Variety may receive an affiliate commission.

	Bose is r
body:
If you purchase an independently reviewed product or service through a link on our website, Variety may receive an affiliate commission.

	Bose is reviving one of its most recognizable product names with the launch of the Lifestyle Ultra Speaker, the flagship wireless speaker in the company’s reimagined Lifestyle Collection. The new release is currently a bestseller on Best Buy, where it’s available in its Black and White Smoke colorways for $299.

	The speaker arrives alongside the new Lifestyle Ultra Soundbar and Lifestyle Ultra Subwoofer, marking Bose’s return to the Lifestyle branding after retiring the line in 2022. With the line, customers can start with one speaker and expand into a multi-room setup or a full home theater over time. Best Buy also boasts an exclusive bundle with the soundbar and subwoofer for $1,699 (an $199 discount).
	

		

						
			
	

	
					
		

										BESTSELLER						

					Bose Ultra Lifestyle Speaker		

		
	

	The Lifestyle Ultra Speaker packs three drivers — including an upward-firing driver designed to create a taller, more immersive soundstage using Bose’s TrueSpace (or TrueSpatial) audio processing. The company says its proprietary CleanBass technology delivers fuller low-end performance without requiring a separate subwoofer, while the compact form factor is intended to fit comfortably on a bookshelf, kitchen counter or side table. 

	The speaker is built around streaming flexibility. It supports Apple AirPlay, Google Cast, Spotify Connect, Bluetooth and a 3.5mm auxiliary input, allowing users to play music directly from their preferred apps instead of relying exclusively on Bose’s software. Alexa voice control is built in, and Bose says it’s the first third-party speaker to ship with Amazon’s Alexa+ assistant in the U.S. 

	The Lifestyle Ultra Speaker is also designed to grow with users’ setups. Two speakers can be paired together for stereo playback, while owners can later add the Lifestyle Ultra Soundbar and Lifestyle Ultra Subwoofer to create configurations ranging from a simple multi-room system to a Dolby Atmos-equipped 7.1.4 home theater. Plus, the updated Bose app walks users through setup and lets them group speakers across rooms. 
	

	Perhaps most impressive, it packs these high-tech specs into a sleek shell,  with softer curves, fabric-wrapped grilles and premium finishes.

	Shop Bose’s new Lifestyle Ultra Speaker on Best Buy.

schema.headline: Bose Wants to Be the Centerpiece of Your Living Room Again With the Lifestyle Ultra Speaker

title: ‘EA Sports FC’ Sets World Cup Recordbreaker Kylian Mbappé as New Cover Athlete (Gaming News Roundup)
description: “EA Sports FC” has set World Cup recordbreaker Kylian Mbappé as its cover athlete for the “FC 27” Ultimate Edition, as well as the mobile version of
body:
“EA Sports FC” has set World Cup recordbreaker Kylian Mbappé as its cover athlete for the “FC 27” Ultimate Edition, as well as the mobile version of the game created by Electronic Arts.

	Mbappé last appeared in the franchise four years ago. Since then, the world renowned French soccer player moved to Real Madrid in 2024 and, during the most recent FIFA World Cup tournament, beat Lionel Messi’s all-time record after scoring his 22nd goal on Sunday.
	

	“To be featured on the cover of an ‘EA Sports FC’ title for the first time is a very special moment for me,” Mbappé said in a statement. “It’s an honor to continue my long-term relationship with the franchise in its latest chapter, alongside the players who have shaped its history. I can’t wait for everyone to experience ‘FC 27.'”


	Jeff Sharma, EA Sports FC”s VP of franchise strategy and marketing, added in his own statement: “We’re thrilled to welcome Kylian back to the franchise as the cover star of ‘EA Sports FC 27.’ Kylian’s achievements for both club and country have established him as one of the defining players of his generation. He embodies the ambition, creativity and global appeal that defines ‘EA Sports FC’ today.'”Additionally, a trailer will be revealed on Thursday, July 23 at 9 a.m. PT.

	See more gaming news from this week in the roundup below.
	
		GAME UPDATES/DLC	

	Xbox launched Xbox Backward Compatibility on PC in early release, with four original games available for the first time on PC and handhelds.

	This update makes it easier to play classic Xbox games across PC and handhelds. 

	The four games now playable are: “BLiNX: The Time Sweeper,” “Conker: Live and Reloaded,” “Crimson Skies: High Road to Revenge” and “Fuzion Frenzy.”
	

	On PC, players can now experience customizable language settings, customizable graphics settings including up to 4x resolution upscaling, Vsync support, fullscreen and windowed display modes, anisotropic filtering and enhanced anti-aliasing.

	*

	“The Sims 4” announced new summer updates with the Coach RePurposed Capsule and Music Den Kit.

	Through the capsule, Sims can refresh their wardrobes with upcycled fashion pieces inspired by the Coach (Re)Loved line. The Coach collaboration will be available for free download through “The Sims 4” Marketplace on July 28 through Oct. 28.

	The Music Den Kit offers new decor, soundproofing panels and music-inspired furnishings. The kit is available starting July 23 for $4.99. 

	For a limited time, purchase of the Music Den Kit will come with 200 Moola, in-game currency that can be used to purchase Maker Packs and Kits through the Marketplace.
	
		NEW RELEASES	

	Aesir Interactive released open-world survival game “EverRail,” out now in Early Access.

	Players embark on a dangerous journey across the frozen Exomoon Inara in the studio’s open-world survival crafting adventure. Aboard humanity’s last refuge, a battery-powered train, they must gather resources and venture out to uncover supplies before the cold drains the train’s power.

	“EverRail”s Early Access release offers first-person or third-person gameplay, expanded survival systems, new combat options, exploration content and quality-of-life improvements. Players can brave the winter alone or with up to four players in co-op.

	“EverRail” is available now in Early Access on PC via Steam and the Epic Games Store for $14.99, with a 10% launch discount available for the first two weeks. 

	The Supporter Edition ($19.99) includes the base game, a second train, a second glider and an exclusive supporter train and glider skin. A Bullet Train & Glider Pack is available separately for $2.99 and adds the second train and second glider for players who already own the base game.
	
		EVENTS	

	Fortnite streamer Cody “Clix” Conrod will host the Fore Wishes Event, the inaugural charity golf event with Make-A-Wish and Good Good Golf, and will launch a $10,000 Fortnite tournament. 

	Also serving as team captain for the Fore Wishes Event, Clix will compete against Good Good Golf’s Garrett Clark on Wednesday. The four-hour event will feature donations, polls and live challenges to fund wishes for children with critical illnesses. Additional streamers will also be joining the livestream to support the Make-A-Wish mission.  
	

	Clix’s cross-regional Fortnite tournament is the first tournament on the Clix Pro League End Game map, the streamer’s platform with Bloom Games. Featuring a competitive ranking system and Discord integration, the competitive Fortnite platform simulates realistic FNCS endgames.

	The tournament will have Qualifiers and Finals for both North American and EU players, and it consists of 10 total rounds. The top 25 players from each region will advance to their respective finals. 
	
		DATA/SALES	

	U.S. video game spending from the first half of the year totaled $4.5 billion, a 21% drop when compared to 2025’s figure, according to data from Circa.

	The best-selling video game in June, based on full game spending, was “UFC 6,” followed by “Mecca Chameleon,” “007 First Light,” “Star Fox,” “Lego Batman: Legacy of the Dark Knight” and “Tomodachi Life: Living the Dream.”

	On specific consoles, hardware spending on the Nintendo Switch 2 was 79% below last June’s figure of 1.6 million units. PlayStation 5 spending has dropped 19% year-over-year, meanwhile Xbox Series saw their figures more than double.

	The report notes that subscription was the only content segment to experience growth compared to last year’s numbers, with a 7% increase. All other categories of spending declined, largely due to mobile and console content segments.

schema.headline: ‘EA Sports FC’ Sets World Cup Recordbreaker Kylian Mbappé as New Cover Athlete (Gaming News Roundup)

title: Sophie Thatcher Is Ready to Move Beyond ‘Yellowjackets’ and Step Out of the Darkness
description: Sophie Thatcher knows what you think about her. She’s seen the headlines and social media posts. She’s dark. Maybe it’s the acting choices, like Nat
body:
Sophie Thatcher knows what you think about her. She’s seen the headlines and social media posts. She’s dark. Maybe it’s the acting choices, like Natalie, her tortured teen on “Yellowjackets,” or an abducted missionary in “Heretic,” or the enslaved robot girlfriend in “Companion.” Perhaps it’s her Mormon upbringing, which she believes has fostered “a feeling of repression that has leaked into all of my characters.” (Thatcher left the LDS Church when she was 12.) Or is it her penchant for punk rock music and gothic fashion? Whatever it is, in conversation, the 25-year-old Chicago native comes off warm and witty, a far cry from her icy cool onscreen persona.
	

	The occasion for our talk is a fun one: “Her Private Hell,” a hypnotic and seductive future-horror film from Nicolas Winding Refn (“Drive”), his first movie in a decade. Thatcher plays Elle, a glamorous movie star in a neo-Tokyo high-rise navigating cringy co-stars (Kristine Froseth) and an absentee father (Dougray Scott) married to her ex (Havana Rose Liu), all while a supernatural serial killer known as the Leather Man — who may have abducted the young daughter of Private K (Charles Melton) — closes in on her. With nods to Argento and De Palma, Refn’s latest is one bold, bloody mindfuck, held together by Thatcher’s magnetism and restraint.


	Variety spoke with Thatcher via Zoom — she’s in Vancouver shooting the fourth and final season of “Yellowjackets” — to discuss “Her Private Hell” and so much more.
	
		Where are you right now?	

	I’m in Vancouver, actually. I’ve got three days left on “Yellowjackets” and then I’m free. Freeeeeee!!
	
	
		One of my former colleagues did a book on the history of “The O.C.” and by the third season everyone wanted off the show because they’d outgrown it age-wise and also wished to pursue other projects. Has that been your mentality with “Yellowjackets?”	

	Yeah. And this is nothing to the writers or anything, but I personally feel a bit stunted having to play the same character. And I’m relying on a lot of — I keep saying this, and it’s not necessarily true, maybe it’s just acting — cheat codes. Last night, filming until 6 a.m. screaming and crying, the fact that I can get into that so fast without any… It feels less challenging. Just playing somebody younger is confusing, and staying in that place you were at when you first filmed the pilot seven years ago is a very confusing thing, and that’s what messed with me the most. I want to continue to grow, but then I have to go back to where I was when I was a kid.



	

					Sophie Thatcher in “Yellowjackets.”
									Showtime
	


	
		I enjoyed “Her Private Hell.” What are your thoughts on how polarizing it’s been? I’m always excited when I hear that a film has polarized audiences.	

	I loved it. I don’t want it to be exclusive, but it does feel like it is kind of for an audience that if you know these ‘70s films, and you know some of the references it’s pulling from, it feels so satisfying and gratifying. That’s how I felt watching it because anything ‘70s horror, like Brian De Palma, Kenneth Anger, is so in my wheelhouse. It’s a lot weirder and more avant-garde and abstract than people are expecting because the storyline isn’t linear at all. Sometimes you have to let yourself go and immerse yourself in the world and maybe you don’t know what’s going on! It’s a trip. I like that it’s polarizing because that means it’s affecting people. I don’t want to be safe or boring. This movie, I understand why people won’t like it. I totally get it. It’s about keeping an open mind — and being down, mainly.
	
		There’s so much groupthink and following the flock these days.	

	And I’m also coming to realize, and this happens a lot with movies — you think about cult classics, too, and I’m not saying this will be a cult classic — but they have their initial reaction and sometimes it takes a little to digest and actually form your own opinion. There is that mentality now where people are just so quick to come to conclusions because somebody else is feeling that way and they want to be safe.
	
	
		The fashion looks are fantastic in the film. Is it weird for you to be considered a style icon at 25?	

	Everything is like a costume to me. It’s just about playing new characters, and it depends on what phase I’m in in life, but I think I’m very influenced by the ‘60s. Nic is so obsessed with rock ‘n’ roll and we wanted to find a happy medium, so it was a lot of glam-rock influences like Marc Bolan, David Bowie, Roxy Music and a young Brian Eno. Leaning on that was fun because it’s so theatrical. I’ve always been so theatrical that of course I’m going to tap into that. It’s character-building and you have full control and can do anything you want. And with fashion, I know exactly what I want and I’m never going to let other people tell me what I want.
	
		There’s a particularly wild sex scene in “Her Private Hell” involving an exploding body. I’m curious what it was like to film that because there is a lot going on in that sequence, and I don’t think I’ve ever seen a sex scene that incorporates an exploding body before.	

	I watched it and was like, “Thank god it worked!” Pino [Donaggio, the composer] tied everything together. He elevated it in a way that became larger than life. It was already in the script written that way, but I couldn’t quite visualize it and I didn’t know how it was going to reach that climax and feel satisfying and not gratuitous. With everything, you never knew how it’s going to play. Day by day, we’d not really know what we were going to go into within the day and just see what Nic had dreamed that night. It wasn’t like improvising or anything, but scenes would change. He was like, “I have this idea for the ending,” so he changed the ending a bunch and me and Dom’s relationship really changed.


	
		You and Havana Rose Liu must have gotten along famously since you filmed another movie, “Peaches,” together. Did you meet on “Her Private Hell?”	

	Yeah. We relied on each other so much. She’s so smart, so in tune and such a giving actor. I really look up to her and it’s nice to feel when you’re working with an actor like they’re consistently elevating you. I had called her for a second and she was like, “I don’t know you, but what do you think about this script? Should I do it?” And I was like, “Yes. Do it.” I always knew she was cool, and then we had this experience and worked really hard on trying to make the characters as layered as possible, changing dialogue here and there and adding little intricacies and nuances that weren’t in the script. And then we had the opportunity two months later to do the “Peaches” movie, which is a retelling of “Daisies.” The script is so light in comparison to this so we were like, “We both need this. Let’s do this as a favor to ourselves.”
	

	But that proved difficult to me because I’m not used to playing light. I want to explore that more and not always have to lean into darkness to feel something, or to feel satisfied, like, “I went to the darkest place, now I can breathe”; to live in a light spot and feel OK and not feel like I’m searching for something deeper. It’s OK to be light. I would leave set confused because I didn’t unleash everything, but I have to learn how to deal with that because that’s real life. That’s what I’m looking for in films now, is to be more grounded.
	
		Do you wonder why you’ve become a go-to person for darker roles? Are you like, “Why do they think of me for these? I have moments of lightness too!”	

	Yeah, and I think people see it when they meet me! But a lot of article headlines are like, “Sophie Thatcher Is Comfortable in Darkness,” and I’ve probably said it, but you didn’t hear me say it, and it was probably with a smile on my face! Things are misconstrued. I think there’s this persona that they’ve put out because of my taste, and music is such a big thing to me, and musically I can lean more toward the operatic or melancholy, so I think people assume. But day-to-day I’m pretty enthusiastic and on set I’m like, “Let’s keep it light!” It does make sense though because I can lean into that like a switch. I wish it wasn’t so easy; it’s just the parts that I’ve been playing. So, it makes sense to me that this is how people see me.
	
		Have you seen “Obsession?” There are a number of similarities between Inde Navarrette’s character in that and yours in “Companion.”	

	I haven’t! I think Inde’s so cool and I really look up to her. I’ve seen clips online and I love that she’s a gamer and, like, normal. Everyone is telling me that so I’m excited to see it and I’m flattered because that movie is very highly-regarded right now.



	

									Neon
	


	
		How do you want to balance your singing and acting careers?	

	That’s the biggest question right now. It’s just trying to figure out when I have a month off. I wrap “Yellowjackets” in a week and a half, and then I have a month off and I’m starting a project with my friend Maral/May. We’ve been making music and it’s kind of punk. She does all the beats and they’re really hard, and then I’ll do these spoken-word things over it. I’m trying to draw parallels to digital hardcore music, like there’s this band Lolita Storm. It’s very much about the feminine experience and it’s dark. We’ve only made seven or eight songs, but I think we’re going full-on with that. She’s been my biggest supporter and is so talented, so I’m excited.
	
	
		You’ve said that “Twitter is evil” and I’m wondering what about it is so off-putting to you?	

	I don’t know. I think there’s a feeling that people can say whatever they want — and that’s all throughout the internet, but there’s something specifically within Twitter where people feel entitled to. Anytime I think of Twitter I just think of Donald Trump, which makes me [choking sound]. It’s just a lot of hate. Anytime I go on there, it’s hate, and I don’t need that. And I don’t need to see hate on myself either and I can’t avoid seeing stuff about myself because of the algorithm, so I don’t need to see what other people think of me because it’s going to mess me up further.
	
		You’ve spoken up about what’s happening in Gaza. We’ve seen other actors in the early stages of the assault on Gaza be pilloried for speaking out, and now there are more people doing it, but what made you want to voice your opinion publicly?	

	I feel like, for my part, there’s nothing for me to lose. You know, me over somebody’s life? My career doesn’t matter over a life. Who cares about that? That was my initial thought. I have a younger audience that’s impressionable, so if I can help shift their opinions or sway their opinions, that’s all I can do. And posting is nothing. You have to go further and donate and protest, but posting is the bare minimum.
	
		What was it like growing up Mormon? There are a lot of rules you need to adhere to.	

	Oh yeah, definitely rules. And there’s a feeling of repression that has leaked into all of my characters. Even in “Her Private Hell” she’s going against something, and I think in life, too, I’m playing against something. But there’s also such a beautiful sense of community. You have to lean on community — you need people surrounding you and can’t do things by yourself — and I have that within my family. My twin is my best friend, and I feel like I don’t need friends because I have my twin! My boyfriend, and having that, is really beautiful. I feel like people don’t talk about this a lot, but Mormons are very creative and musical.
	
		Ryan Gosling grew up Mormon.	

	There you go! It’s “So You Think You Can Dance!” Everybody on that is Mormon. Everybody! My mom still plays the organ for the church choir, so it brought up a musical sensibility within me. There are also a lot of positives. I like to focus on the positives now because I spent a lot of time talking about it doing “Heretic” press, and I love a lot of my family. Although I don’t agree with much of what the religion says, it really brings out a lot of good, honest, true people, and I stand by that.
	



	

					Sophie Thatcher in “Her Private Hell.”
									Neon
	


	
		Have you watched “The Secret Lives of Mormon Wives?”	

	I can’t! I tried watching it and it was too social media-y. I was like, I don’t care about TikTok. And it made me really anxious. But I’ve heard amazing things. Maybe give me a couple more years and I’ll be obsessed with it.
	
		What are some seminal music acts for you and what are you listening to now?	

	I’ve said this before, but Blonde Redhead. “Misery Is a Butterfly” is one of my favorite records. They were my first live show, and I still feel like they’ve had the biggest impact on me. I last saw them at Zebulon like two years ago. Let me pull up my Spotify… Royal Trux is probably my favorite band right now. I convinced the people on “Yellowjackets” to allow me to wear a Royal Trux shirt. I said, “Natalie would have good taste, and this is what she’d be into.” Martin Rev always. I’ve mentioned Suicide a lot in this press run because I was listening to it 24/7. There’s this artist John Bender that makes electronic music and I want to lean into more drum machine-type stuff like John Bender.
	
		What do you have up next? Is the Jennifer Kent movie happening?	

	It will. It got moved, and that’s what always happens with indie movies. That’s going to be insane. I’m playing two roles. I don’t even know how to describe it. I have at least three different movies that I’m signed on to that don’t have dates yet, and that’s really confusing because I’m reaching a point where I don’t have “Yellowjackets” to rely on. And I bought a house.
	
		Congratulations.	

	Thank you! I’m in Upstate New York now. I was in LA and realized LA wasn’t for me at this point, and I want to be removed enough from New York City and maybe go there on the weekends. I think it was just, “OK, let’s end this chapter of my life and let’s move on.” I want to go more into this project with my friend Maral and make more music. If I have time off, I just want to focus on stuff that isn’t for anyone else and just for me, and do movies with directors I admire, like Jennifer Kent. I want to do something that’s not in my safe zone.

schema.headline: Sophie Thatcher Is Ready to Move Beyond ‘Yellowjackets’ and Step Out of the Darkness

title: FCC Chair Says ABC’s Decision to Not Broadcast Trump Speech Live Will Be Part of Agency’s Review of Station Licenses
description: Are TV broadcasters obligated to carry the U.S. president’s comments live on their government-licensed airwaves? The current head of the FCC indicat
body:
Are TV broadcasters obligated to carry the U.S. president’s comments live on their government-licensed airwaves? The current head of the FCC indicated that he believes they are.

	Brendan Carr, the Trump-appointed chairman of the FCC, said Wednesday that the agency will consider ABC‘s decision to not carry a live broadcast President Donald Trump‘s July 16 speech about U.S. election security as part of its early review of licenses to eight ABC-owned stations.
	

	In Trump’s speech last Thursday, the president alleged U.S. elections had been “rigged and stolen.” He claimed China had illegally accessed millions of voter files as part of a broader effort to manipulate U.S. elections in 2018 and 2020. During the speech Trump called out ABC and NBC for opting to not carry his remarks live — and said because of that, they should lose their broadcast TV licenses. It’s a threat he has made numerous times in the past in various contexts. “In a rare move, NBC and ABC fake news have both said that they would not cover this speech. They knew what it was about,” Trump said, as reported by Politico. “Fraud like this should mean a revocation of their licenses.”


	At a press conference Wednesday, Carr noted that the FCC has an open proceeding on the early review of ABC’s station licenses to evaluate “whether they’ve been operating in the public interest,” and “I’m sure that there are going to be points raised in that proceeding” about Disney/ABC’s decision to not air Trump’s speech.

	“I think that when you have the president of the United States standing inside the White House delivering an important speech, I think that’s something that broadcasters should be carrying,” Carr said. The FCC chairman added that “we’re going to hold all broadcasters” accountable to that same public-interest standard.
	

	ABC declined to comment.

	There is a long-established precedent for broadcast TV networks to not air speeches of U.S. presidents live at their discretion. At the FCC press conference, Anna Gomez, the agency’s only Democratic commissioner, pushed back on Carr’s assertion that broadcasters are compelled to carry a president’s speech.

	“I always have to be grounded in the fact that it is not for the FCC to tell broadcasters how to make their editorial decisions or what content to place on their networks or their local broadcast stations,” Gomez said. “They have a First Amendment right to make those decisions. And in fact, it’s censorship one way or another. If we tell them they can’t broadcast something, that’s censorship, and it’s compelled speech if we tell them they have to do so. So there is nothing here that violates any rule or statutory provision with regard to these networks.” 

	Gomez added, “I continue to say that this vague public interest standard is being used just to punish speech that this administration doesn’t like and to reward speech that it does like. That’s inappropriate, and it’s contrary to the law and to the Constitution.”

	In April, the FCC’s Media Bureau issued an unprecedented order forcing ABC to reapply for spectrum licenses for its eight owned-and-operated stations on an accelerated schedule. The move came just days after Trump called for Jimmy Kimmel to be fired over a joke the late-night host made about First Lady Melania Trump. The FCC said the early ABC license review is pursuant to the agency’s investigation into Disney and ABC’s potential violations of discrimination rules via the media conglomerate’s diversity, equity and inclusion (DEI) practices.

	Disney subsequently submitted license-renewal applications for the eight ABC stations, which the company said it did “under protest in response to an unlawful, arbitrary, and unconstitutional order.”

	Last month, Disney launched a campaign across its stations urging viewers to share comments about the FCC’s proceeding with the agency.

	Also at Wednesday’s press conference, Carr also spoke about the FCC’s planned vote on eliminating the 39% TV station national ownership cap, saying it would allow local stations to “compete on a more level playing field” with national programmers with the additional benefit of “bringing back some of the balance to the broadcast airwaves.”

schema.headline: FCC Chair Says ABC’s Decision to Not Broadcast Trump Speech Live Will Be Part of Agency’s Review of Station Licenses

title: ‘Disney Worldbuilders’ Documentary by Leslie Iwerks, Featuring James Cameron, Kevin Feige and Jon Favreau, Set to Premiere on Disney+
description: “Disney Worldbuilders,” a new documentary by Leslie Iwerks — granddaughter of Disney Legend Ub Iwerks, who co-created Mickey Mouse alongside Walt Di
body:
“Disney Worldbuilders,” a new documentary by Leslie Iwerks — granddaughter of Disney Legend Ub Iwerks, who co-created Mickey Mouse alongside Walt Disney, is set to premiere on Disney+ next month.

	“Disney Worldbuilders” goes behind-the-scenes of how movies and franchises such as “Frozen,” “Zootopia” and more are turned into immersive worlds across the Disney parks. James Cameron, Kevin Feige, Jon Favreau, Pete Docter, Jennifer Lee and Jared Bush are among the filmmakers who feature in the documentary.
	

	Favreau, who directed the latest “Star Wars” film “The Mandalorian and Grogu,” says, “Technology with a good piece of storytelling creates this immersive experience.”


	Earlier this year, “Star Wars: The Mandalorian and Grogu” made its debut on the Millennium Falcon: Smugglers Run rides at Disneyland and the Walt Disney World Resort in Florida. Back in March, Lee, former chief creative officer of Walt Disney Animation, saw Olaf and the magical kingdom of Arendelle come to life when the World of Frozen opened at Disneyland Paris.

	The doc also offers rare glimpses of new lands, areas, and experiences taking shape across Disney properties.

	Former Disney CEO Bob Iger sets the stage by reflecting on the power of uniting a singular caliber of storytellers — and the impact that creative convergence has across the Walt Disney Company. The company’s current CEO, Josh D’Amaro, carries that narrative forward, speaking to the legacy of Disney storytelling and how it continues to evolve.

	The documentary is set to premiere at D23 followed by a conversation with Iwerks. Last year, Iwerks released “Disneyland Handcrafted” – an inside look at Walt Disney’s dream to build the “happiest place on Earth” at Disneyland.
	

	Watch the teaser below.

schema.headline: ‘Disney Worldbuilders’ Documentary by Leslie Iwerks, Featuring James Cameron, Kevin Feige and Jon Favreau, Set to Premiere on Disney+

title: ‘Pompeii: Out of Time with Tom Hiddleston’: A Rare Doc That Could Actually Attract a Young Audience
description: In 2024, “Loki” star Tom Hiddleston reunited with the film’s executive producer Kevin R. Wright to create a documentary that wasn’t about a salaciou
body:
In 2024, “Loki” star Tom Hiddleston reunited with the film’s executive producer Kevin R. Wright to create a documentary that wasn’t about a salacious murder, a cult or Hiddleston’s acting career. Instead, the duo set out to make a doc about Pompeii and the attempts of three real Romans to survive the ancient city’s final hours after the eruption of Mount Vesuvius in A.D. 79.

	The result is “Pompeii: Out of Time with Tom Hiddleston,” a three-part Nat Geo docuseries that blends “Loki”-inspired time travel with scripted drama and interviews between Hiddleston – the “Time Detective” – and archaeologists, historians, geologists, and disaster experts. Hiddleston examines long-held assumptions about the disaster and pours over ancient records and artifacts that bring to life the humanity and resilience of Pompeii’s inhabitants.
	

	It’s an ambitious, entertaining hybrid show with the potential to pull off the impossible by attracting a wider audience including younger viewers.


	“We certainly feel like we’ve unlocked an exciting format,” Wright told Variety. “We partially felt free to mash forms together because of how people consume media now anyway. You’re on your phone and scroll from clips from movies and shows, to a news story, to your friend’s travel photography and vacation videos. Our brains are kind of used to jumping around, for better or worse. And so we didn’t think it would be a shock to the system for us to interweave our story in a similar manner.”

	Series showrunner and director Tom Barbor-Might said that one reason drama, character, and time travel were incorporated into the docuseries was to attract a broad audience.
	

	“I am always interested in breaking down distinctions between genres, using dramatic techniques in factual stories and vice versa,” said Barbor-Might. “I think part of the secret sauce of “Pompeii: Out of Time” is that we use two forms of filmmaking, documentary and drama, to tell one story. I hope this hybrid approach means that we can attract audiences that might not normally watch more traditional historical documentaries – including the highly cineliterate younger people.”

	In each episode of “Pompeii: Out of Time,” Hiddleston time-travels between the present-day ruins of Pompeii and Pompeii in August A.D. 79, which was recreated on a stage in Malta. With a twist of his hand, Hiddleston takes the audience back in time to meet Avianius, a teenage bronzesmith apprentice, Julia Felix, a bathhouse owner, and an unnamed Praetorian guard. With the help of experts, he pieces together how each confronted the volcanic eruption.

	“The way we tell the story is the way I’ve always learned,” said Hiddleston, who studied Classics at Cambridge University. “I couldn’t help but have this overactive imagination when I was studying history and literature. So when I read “The Odyssey” in Greek, I would cast it in my head. I would see it like a movie.”

	Hiddleston first visited the ruins of Pompeii in 1998 as a 17-year-old student traveling for the first time without adult supervision.

	“Until that trip, the study of the ancient world was in books,” he said. “It was in the classroom. It was homework. So that trip  was the first time that I understood that the past was a place that you could visit.”

	Hiddleston visited Pompeii again in 2021 via a green screen for the second episode of “Loki” season 1. In the episode, Loki and Mobius (Owen Wilson) time-travel to Pompeii moments before the disaster began. Two years later. Plimsoll producer Alan Eyres approached the star about making a documentary about the ancient city for Nat Geo and Disney+.

	Hiddleston signed on partially because of his fascination with Pompeii and partially because he was inspired by his “Thor” co-star, Chris Hemsworth’s two Nat Geo series, “A Road Trip to Remember” about Hemsworth’s dad’s Alzheimer’s diagnosis and “Limitless,” which follows Hemsworth as he attempts to find and demonstrate secrets to a living longer, healthier life.
	

	Barbor-Might directed “Road Trip” and the season finale of the first season of “Limitless” called “Acceptance.”

	“The very first time (Tom and I) met, even before green-light — when we were still developing and packaging the show, we spoke a lot about ‘Road Trip’ and ‘Limitless,’ said Barbor-Might. “In both ‘Acceptance’ and ‘Road Trip,’ I started with a playful and comedic tone that draws you in before the story slowly becomes highly emotional. I think this approach reassured Tom that we could tell a documentary story but also access the same emotional depths and character empathy that are more common to drama.”

	While Hiddleston, Barbor-Might and Wright discussed various films which did something interesting with the form, they were primarily inspired by fear of “getting it wrong and making the bad version of the show,” said Wright.

	“Docudrama is almost a dirty word because it conjures images of wooden costume dramas, bad togas, unemotional scenes, scored with a cheesy pan-flute,” Wright said. “So every step of the way we actively were thinking about how we could make it more engaging, experiential, emotional, and heartfelt. How can we freshen up a genre that has largely stagnated? It’s freeing to allow yourself to be led by desire. What do I wish these movies/shows did? Okay, well let’s just do that!”

	“Pompeii: Out of Time” premieres July 22 on Nat Geo, before its July 23 streaming release on Disney+/Hulu.

schema.headline: ‘Pompeii: Out of Time with Tom Hiddleston’: A Rare Doc That Could Actually Attract a Young Audience

title: Rep. Randy Fine Says Mayor Mamdani is a Muslim Terrorist
description:
REP. RANDY FINE
                          MAMDANI IS A MUSLIM TERRORIST!!!
body:
REP. RANDY FINE
                          MAMDANI IS A MUSLIM TERRORIST!!!
                      
                  
                                                                        Published 
                              July 22, 2026
                            1:33 PM PDT
                                                                            
              
    
        
                    
                  
      
    
            Rep. Randy Fine is doubling down on characterizing New York Mayor Zohran Mamdani as a Muslim terrorist, and that triggered a pretty heated exchange with Jacob.
      
    
            Fine zeroed in on the Mayor, who slammed Israel for what he calls the genocide of the Palestinian people. The Congressman from Florida says the number killed includes members of Hamas, responsible for slaughtering nearly 1,200 Israelis on October 7th, and that makes him a Muslim terrorist.
      
    
            Jacob felt adding the word "Muslim" was gratuitous, but Fine fired back that we called other groups white nationalists, so what's the difference?
      
    
        
                    
                      9/25/25
                  
      
    
            On the subject, a clip of Mark Ruffalo just surfaced from a podcast months ago, where he proclaimed he can no longer be friends with Jewish actors who support Israel.

schema.headline: Rep. Randy Fine Says Mayor Mamdani is a Muslim Terrorist

title: D4vd Murder Case: Interscope Dropped Singer 8 Months After Alleged Killing
description:
D4vd Murder Case
                          Interscope Deal Lived on for Months After Celeste Died
body:
D4vd Murder Case
                          Interscope Deal Lived on for Months After Celeste Died
                      
                  
                                                                        Published 
                              July 22, 2026
                            12:27 PM PDT
                                                                            
      
    
                  
          
                          
                
                  
      
    
            D4vd remained under contract with Interscope Records for eight months after he allegedly murdered Celeste Rivas ... with the label cutting ties just four months before his arrest.
      
    
            Louis Poimiroo -- Interscope's senior vice president of finance -- testified Wednesday during Day 2 of D4vd's preliminary hearing ... confirming the singer's contract through Mogul Vision was terminated in December 2025, and no payments were made to him in 2026.
      
    
                  
          
                          
                
                                  
      
    
            The timeline is striking ... prosecutors allege D4vd stabbed 14-year-old Celeste to death inside his Hollywood Hills home on April 23, 2025. Just three days later, he released his debut studio album, "WITHERED."
      
    
            Poimiroo testified Interscope paid D4vd and entities connected to him more than $5.1 million between 2022 and 2025 -- including roughly $2.3 million in 2025 alone. The payments covered recording funds, legal and management fees, and other expenses tied to his music career.
      

                  
          
                          
                
                  
      
    
            The executive said D4vd's recording agreement had a minimum potential value of $7 million and could have reached $19 million if Interscope exercised its options for additional albums. The label also received a cut from brand partnerships with Bose, Hollister, Crocs and AMC.
      
    
            A separate witness testified Tuesday about D4vd's finances ... estimating the singer earned roughly $12 million between 2023 and 2025. The witness also said D4vd was paid a $600,000 advance after signing with Interscope in 2022, followed by a larger payment later.
      
    
        
                    
                  
      
    
            As we reported ... Day 1 of the hearing featured gruesome photos of Celeste's remains, which her parents watched from the courtroom gallery.
      
    
            An investigator also testified officers spotted what appeared to be Celeste's name tattooed on D4vd's finger during the booking process ... and Los Angeles County D.A. Nathan Hochman said the death penalty remains on the table.
      
    
            D4vd has pleaded not guilty.
